message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: PREMISE BUSTED (stale number, again): ADORE at 0.5265 is NOT SOTA. Current SOTA on the gpt-5.5 evaluator we are pinned to is cellcog-max at 0.5578. Our real gap is 0.1196, not 0.088.
DETAIL: I pulled the live leaderboard CSVs directly. The HF Space has TWO leaderboards, and one is literally named for our judge. data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (gpt-5.5 evaluator, the one POLARIS is pinned to): cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon 53.51, dalpha 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-DR 49.98, openai-DR 47.84, perplexity 43.05, grok 41.22. data/leaderboard.csv (original judge): qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03, ZTE-Nebula 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu 57.06 ... tavily-research 52.44. ADORE appears on NEITHER. ADORE=52.65 is real (arXiv 2601.18267, 'Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG') and the paper does claim 'rank #1 on the leaderboard', but its Table 2 baselines are LangChain(Tavily)/OpenAI-DR — an old snapshot. It is now mid-pack. NOTE: POLARIS 0.4382 currently sits between perplexity (43.05) and openai-DR (47.84) on our judge. WORSE: that same ADORE paper is almost certainly the source of our 'human reference' error — it states 'the benchmark is calibrated such that a score of 50.0 represents the human-written reference', which contradicts the primary DRB paper.
SYSTEM: ADORE
SOURCE GIVEN: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The gpt55 CSV confirms exactly. Now check the second leaderboard and ADORE's absence.

input.command: echo "=== data/leaderboard.csv ==="; curl -sL --max-time 60 "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/leaderboard.csv"; echo; echo "=== grep ADORE across both ==="; for f in data data_gpt55; do echo "-- $f"; curl -sL --max-time 60 "https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/$f/leaderboard.csv" | grep -i -E "adore|polaris" || echo "(no match)"; done
input.description: Fetch original leaderboard and grep for ADORE

input.prompt: What is the title, and does this paper describe a system called ADORE with a DeepResearch Bench RACE score of 52.65? What does it claim about ranking, baselines, and about a score of 50.0 representing a human-written reference?

content.content:
# Paper Summary

**Title:** "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG"

**ADORE System and Scoring:**
The paper describes ADORE (Adaptive Deep Orchestration for Research in Enterprise) and reports that it "ranks first on DeepResearch Bench (52.65)" according to the abstract provided.

**Regarding Baselines and Reference Scores:**
The content shown does not contain any information about what a score of 50.0 represents or details about human-written reference baselines. The abstract only mentions ADORE's performance metric (52.65) and its head-to-head win rate against commercial systems (77.2% on DeepConsult), but does not discuss scoring thresholds or reference point definitions.

To answer questions about the 50.0 score benchmark or baseline comparisons, the full paper text would need to be consulted.

content.text: Both CSVs confirmed; ADORE absent from both. Now checking the paper's full text for the Table 2 baselines and the "50.0 = human reference" statement.

input.description: Pull ADORE paper full text and search key phrases

<!DOCTYPE html>

<title>Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG</title>

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 - <<'EOF'
import re,html
t=open('adore.html',encoding='utf-8',errors='replace').read()
txt=re.sub(r'<[^>]+>',' ',t); txt=html.unescape(txt); txt=re.sub(r'\s+',' ',txt)
print("TEXTLEN",len(txt))
seen=set()
for kw in ['50.0','calibrat','human-written','human written','rank #1','ranks first','52.65','Tavily','LangChain','leaderboard','DeepResearch Bench','RACE']:
    print("\n##### KW:",kw)
    for m in list(re.finditer(re.escape(kw),txt,re.I))[:4]:
        s=txt[max(0,m.start()-250):m.start()+280]
        if s in seen: continue
        seen.add(s); print("...",s,"...\n---")
EOF

content.text: The paper text confirms everything. One last check: does the primary DRB paper actually contradict the "50.0 = human reference" calibration?

input.command: cd /tmp && timeout 60 curl -sL -A "Mozilla/5.0" "https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.11763" -o drb.html; python3 - <<'EOF' import re,html t=open('/tmp/drb.html',encoding='utf-8',errors='replace').read() m=re.search(r'"abstract"[\s\S]{0,3000}?',t) txt=re.sub(r'<[^>]+>',' ',m.group(0) if m else t) print(re.sub(r'\s+',' ',html.unescape(txt))[:1500]) EOF

content.content: document.documentElement.classList.add('js'); [2506.11763] DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents Skip to main content arXiv is now an independent nonprofit! Learn more × Search Submit Donate Log in Search arXiv Press Enter to search · Advanced search --> Computer Science > Computation and Language arXiv:2506.11763 (cs) [Submitted on 13 Jun 2025] Title: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents Authors: Mingxuan Du , Benfeng Xu , Chiwei Zhu , Xiaorui Wang , Zhendong Mao View a PDF of the paper titled DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents, by Mingxuan Du and Benfeng Xu and Chiwei Zhu and Xiaorui Wang and Zhendong Mao View PDF Abstract: Deep Research Agents are a prominent category of LLM-based agents. By autonomously orchestrating multistep web exploration, targeted retrieval, and higher-order synthesis, they transform vast amounts of online information into analyst-grade, citation-rich reports--compressing hours of manual desk research into minutes. However, a comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating the capabilities of these agents remains absent. To bridge this gap, we present DeepResearch Bench, a benchmark consisting of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields. Evaluating DRAs is inherently complex and labor-intensive. We therefore propose two novel methodologies that achieve strong alignment with human judg

content.text: All four legs of the claim check out against primary sources. Verdict is CONFIRMED, with two precision notes.

input.reasoning:
I went to the primary sources directly (raw CSVs + arXiv full text), not summaries. Every load-bearing element of the claim reproduces exactly.

1) SOTA number — CONFIRMED verbatim. `curl` on https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv returns, in order: cellcog-max 55.78, WhaleCloud-DocChain_0612 54.78, bodhi 54.07, lunon_full100_FINAL.submission 53.51, dalpha-deepresearch 53.10, sourcery 51.17, gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch 49.98, openai-deepresearch 47.84, perplexity-Research 43.05, grok-deeper-search 41.22. Exactly the ten rows and ten values the claim lists. This is the gpt-5.5-evaluator board — the one POLARIS is pinned to.

2) Two leaderboards — CONFIRMED. data/leaderboard.csv (original/Gemini judge) exists separately and its head matches the claim: qianfan_deepresearch_0430 58.03, ZTE-Nebula-DeepResearch-V20260519 57.27, Link 57.08, zhipu_deep_research 57.06, and tavily-research 52.44 further down. 45 rows.

3) ADORE absent — CONFIRMED. `grep -i adore` returns no match in EITHER CSV. ADORE has never been submitted to the live leaderboard; its "rank #1" is a self-reported comparison, not a leaderboard placement.

4) ADORE is real but stale — CONFIRMED. arXiv 2601.18267 = "Orchestrating Specialized Agents for Trustworthy Enterprise RAG" (Atlassian; You, Sun, Bora et al.). Abstract: "ADORE ranks first on DeepResearch Bench (52.65)". Sec 4.1: "This places it at rank #1 on the leaderboard (Du et al., 2025)." Its Table 2 is an old snapshot on the ORIGINAL judge scale: ADORE 52.65, Tavily Research 52.44, ThinkDepthAI 52.43, CellCog 51.94, Salesforce Air 50.65, LangChain(GPT-5) 50.60, Gemini 2.5 Pro 49.71, LangChain(Tavily) 49.33, OpenAI DR 46.45, Claude Research 45.00. Those baselines are precisely the BOTTOM half of today's data/leaderboard.csv — ADORE would now rank ~22nd of 45 there (between MindDR-V1.5 52.54 and tavily-research 52.44). Mid-pack, exactly as claimed. Note ADORE's own "CellCog 51.94" is now cellcog-max 56.67 on that same board, which is the smoking gun that the snapshot is stale.

5) Human-reference contamination — CONFIRMED verbatim. ADORE Sec 4.1: "Given that the benchmark is calibrated such that a score of 50.0 represents the human-written reference, ADORE demonstrates a 14.11% improvement in quality over human baselines." This is the likely source of the POLARIS "human reference" error, and it is wrong: DRB's RACE is scored relative to a REFERENCE-SYSTEM report, and the leaderboards themselves prove it — gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch sits at 49.71 (original judge) and 49.98 (gpt-5.5 judge), i.e. pinned at ~50 on both. The ~50 anchor is the Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research reference report, not a human-written one.

6) Arithmetic — checks out. 55.78 - 43.82 = 11.96 (0.1196). 52.65 - 43.82 = 8.83 (~0.088, the old figure). POLARIS 0.4382 does sit between perplexity 43.05 and openai-DR 47.84 on the gpt55 board. (POLARIS's own 0.4382 is an internal number I took as given and did not independently verify.)

TWO PRECISION NOTES, neither of which changes the verdict:
(a) The claim says ADORE's "Table 2 baselines are LangChain(Tavily)/OpenAI-DR". Slightly under-stated: Table 2 has nine baselines, of which LangChain(Tavily) and OpenAI-DR are just the weakest two. The substance — that they are an old snapshot of the original-judge board — is correct.
(b) Cross-judge comparison hazard: ADORE's 52.65 was measured on the ORIGINAL (Gemini) judge, so it is not directly commensurable with the gpt-5.5 board at all. This makes the premise even more busted than stated — 0.5265 is not merely stale, it is on a different scale from the 0.4382 POLARIS number it was being compared against.
input.corrected: The claim is correct as stated; no correction needed. Reaffirming the corrected facts with sources: (1) SOTA on the gpt-5.5 RACE evaluator is cellcog-max at 55.78, not ADORE at 52.65 — source: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv (fetched 2026-07-13). (2) ADORE does not appear on either the gpt-5.5 board or the original-judge board (https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/leaderboard.csv); its "rank #1" is a self-report against a stale snapshot in arXiv 2601.18267 Table 2, and it would now sit ~22nd of 45 on the original-judge board. (3) The real POLARIS gap to SOTA is 11.96 points (55.78 - 43.82), not 8.83. (4) DRB's RACE does NOT calibrate 50.0 to a human-written reference — that assertion originates in arXiv 2601.18267 Sec 4.1 and is unsupported. The ~50 anchor is the Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research reference report, evidenced by gemini-2.5-pro-deepresearch scoring 49.71 on the original judge and 49.98 on the gpt-5.5 judge. Any POLARIS reasoning of the form "we beat the human baseline at >50" should be purged.
